text:
Time moves in one direction. This is the first fact about it, and perhaps the most important. You cannot retrieve yesterday. It is simply gone, and no effort or grief changes this.

People often say time passes quickly when they are happy and slowly when they are in pain. This is roughly accurate but also backwards. Time does not change speed. What changes is how closely you are paying attention to it. Suffering makes you watch the clock. Joy makes you forget it exists.

There is also the question of how much time you have. Nobody knows the answer to this for themselves. You can estimate, you can plan, you can set reminders. But the actual number is hidden. This sounds frightening, and sometimes it is. It is also, if you let it be, clarifying. A resource with an unknown limit tends to focus the mind.

The philosophers have argued about whether time is real or just a useful fiction the mind imposes on events. This is an interesting argument. It does not change how to live inside time, which is the more practical question.

What seems true: the present moment is the only one you can actually do anything in. The past is fixed. The future is uncertain. Right now is where action lives. This is not a new observation. It has been made thousands of times across thousands of years, which itself says something about how hard humans find it to remember.

So: pay attention to what is happening. Do the things that matter. Let the rest go. Time will keep moving regardless. The only variable is what you do with the portion you are given.

That is most of what there is to say about it.
